summary: Former Baja California governor Ruffo's detention in a cartel-linked fuel-smuggling case, with alleged army ties, lands as Sheinbaum faces US scrutiny over Morena governors' suspected cartel links in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora. On trade, Chinese-built trucks operating in Mexico complicate efforts to ease Trump tariffs. Separately, 83% of gas stations now comply with the 27-peso diesel cap, and Supreme Court justices' salaries were cut for a second year.

lead.h1: Former Baja California governor Ruffo detained in cartel-linked case
lead.context: The arrest of the first opposition governor in recent memory lands amid US scrutiny of Morena governors' alleged cartel ties, reshaping the political fight over organized crime.
lead.background: Ernesto Ruffo, Mexico's first opposition governor after defeating the PRI in Baja California in 1989, is now central to a fuel-smuggling probe the government says has run over a year.
lead.drivers: Ruffo's arrest gives Morena political relief during one of its most difficult weeks handling the Baja California and Sinaloa cartel-tie scandals.
lead.implications: The case reframes the corruption narrative toward opposition figures rather than Morena governors, and marks the first time in Sheinbaum's term an opposition figure has been detained.

items.headline: Chinese-made heavy trucks in Mexico complicate bid to ease Trump tariffs
items.context: A growing fleet of China-built trucks operating in Mexico gives Washington another argument to maintain tariff pressure, tying trade relief to scrutiny of Chinese content in Mexico's supply chains.
items.background: USMCA's annual review process now scrutinizes whether Mexico's export platform, especially trucking, is becoming a route for Chinese manufacturers into North America.
items.drivers: Chinese truck import value into Mexico rose more than sevenfold over six years and the number of Chinese truck firms operating there reached about 23, alarming US officials and Mexican manufacturers.
items.implications: The trend complicates Mexican negotiators' push for tariff relief on an integrated export industry, since it undercuts Mexico's case for alignment with the US against China.

items.headline: Sheinbaum faces scrutiny over Morena governors' alleged cartel ties
items.context: Reports point to governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora facing US scrutiny over suspected links to organized crime, testing the government's response as it downplays the allegations.
items.background: Sheinbaum has built her US relationship on a pragmatic, low-friction approach even as Mexico's federal judiciary and states operate under new electoral rules following the 2024 judicial reform.
items.drivers: Leaked audio and reports have widened US scrutiny to governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora, with Baja California's Marina del Pilar Ávila the latest named.
items.implications: The escalating US allegations test the sovereignty-based defense Sheinbaum has used for four months, even as she has managed the Trump relationship carefully.

items.headline: Former governor Ruffo's fuel-smuggling hearing drags on with army links alleged
items.context: The ongoing case against ex-Baja California governor Ruffo includes a businessman accused of running a separate fuel-contraband network involving Defense Ministry personnel, tying the trial to broader concerns about military involvement in huachicol fiscal.
items.background: Huachicol fiscal is the tax-fraud scheme of smuggling fuel while declaring only part of it, and Ruffo was the first opposition politician to unseat the PRI as Baja California governor.
items.drivers: The case also implicates businessman Riestra Fernández, accused of running a separate contraband network involving Defense Ministry personnel, while political rhetoric for and against Ruffo's detention keeps building.
items.implications: The article notes the case matters to both sides: PAN feels bound to defend a historic figure, while Morena, facing its own governors accused of organized-crime ties, gets a chance to shift attention.

items.headline: Mexico says 83% of gas stations now comply with diesel price cap
items.context: The federal government's voluntary ceiling of 27 pesos per liter for diesel has been adopted by most stations, a step meant to contain fuel costs and inflation pressure.
items.background: The 27-peso-per-liter diesel ceiling is a voluntary pact among gas stations, arranged with the energy and finance ministries, Pemex and the consumer protection agency Profeco to keep fuel costs down.
items.implications: Diesel feeds directly into freight and logistics costs, so uneven compliance means transport-cost relief varies sharply by state rather than applying nationwide.

items.headline: Large taxpayers account for nearly half of Mexico's tax revenue
items.context: Corporations with annual revenue above 1,500 million pesos form a small group that funds a large share of public finances, underscoring Mexico's reliance on a concentrated tax base.
items.background: Mexico's tax system runs through SAT, whose Administración General de Grandes Contribuyentes oversees corporations earning over 1,500 million pesos a year, financial entities and state productive companies.
items.drivers: A tiny share of the taxpayer roll, about 0.02%, generates nearly half of federal tax revenue, making this segment central to public finances.
items.implications: The SAT had covered only 29.14% of its import-tax collection goal by the end of May, showing the strain on broadening revenue beyond this small group.

items.headline: Supreme Court justices' salaries cut for second consecutive year
items.context: The reduction in pay and benefits for Mexico's top judges follows a broader push to trim public-sector compensation, extending austerity measures into the judiciary.
items.background: The 2024 judicial reform replaced Supreme Court appointment with election, and pay for the new justices is constitutionally capped so no official earns more than the president.
items.drivers: Official pay tables in the 2026 federal budget cut justices' compensation roughly 35% from the previous court, which had earned about 206,000 pesos net monthly plus benefits.
items.implications: Even after the cut, to up to 191,657 pesos, Supreme Court salaries remain among the highest in the federal public sector.

items.headline: Mexican health authorities investigate cyclosporiasis outbreak tied to Taylor Farms
items.context: Cofepris and Senasica are investigating after US authorities reported 1,644 cases linked to the supplier, prompting preventive measures in Mexico amid cross-border food safety concerns.
items.background: Cofepris and Senasica are Mexico's health and agricultural safety regulators, tasked with inspecting food exporters and responding to cross-border outbreaks.
items.drivers: US authorities reported 1,644 cyclosporiasis cases tied to shredded iceberg lettuce, prompting Taylor Farms to recall products sourced from Mexico.
items.implications: Mexican authorities describe their response as preventive only, with inspections and traceability analysis but no confirmed cause yet.
items.next: A joint technical group has been formed to investigate and adopt further preventive measures.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.52 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.

live.tmpl: the peso trades at {v} pesos to the dollar
